options.A: Multilist fields require a data source configuration
options.B: TreelistEx stores item references as paths
options.C: TreelistEx is more performant than Droplink for single item selection
options.D: Droplist stores item references as GUIDs

questions.explanation: A is correct because multilist fields must have a configured data source for their options. C is correct as TreelistEx is specifically noted as the most efficient field for single item selection. B is incorrect because TreelistEx stores GUIDs, not paths. D is incorrect because Droplist stores paths, not GUIDs.

questions.question: A client wants to implement a new XM Cloud project using JSS-based technologies. Which of the following technology stacks would be fully supported for both editing and rendering?
questions.explanation: Next.js is a fully supported JSS-based technology in XM Cloud that works with both the editing and rendering hosts. ASP.NET MVC Core is supported but not JSS-based and requires custom rendering host implementation. PHP and Python Django are not supported technologies for XM Cloud rendering hosts.

questions.question: Which technologies are supported out-of-the-box for XM Cloud rendering hosts?
questions.explanation: React, ASP.NET MVC Core, and Angular are all supported technologies for XM Cloud rendering hosts. React and Angular are JSS-based technologies that work with the editing host, while ASP.NET MVC Core is supported but requires custom rendering host implementation. Ruby on Rails is not a supported technology for XM Cloud.

questions.question: A developer needs to restrict which components can be added to a placeholder named 'header' across multiple pages. What is the most appropriate approach?

options.A: Create individual placeholder restrictions on each page template
options.B: Configure a single placeholder settings item for 'header'
options.C: Add component restrictions in the layout details of each page
options.D: Create separate placeholder settings for each page's header

questions.explanation: Since placeholder settings have instance-wide scope, creating a single placeholder settings item for 'header' will apply the restrictions to all placeholders with that name across the entire Sitecore instance. Options A, C, and D are inefficient approaches that don't leverage the instance-wide scope of placeholder settings.

questions.question: Which steps are required to properly implement dynamic placeholders in a rendering?

options.A: The rendering's parameters template must inherit from the Dynamic Placeholder base template
options.B: A placeholder settings item must be linked to the rendering's item
options.C: The placeholder name must be unique across the entire Sitecore instance
options.D: The rendering must be added to a allowed controls list

questions.explanation: To configure dynamic placeholders, two key requirements must be met: the rendering's parameters template must inherit from the Dynamic Placeholder base template, and a placeholder settings item must be linked to the rendering's item. Options C and D are incorrect - dynamic placeholders don't require instance-wide unique names, and there's no requirement for an allowed controls list specifically for dynamic placeholders.

questions.question: When configuring a local development environment to work with the cloud-based Pages application, which steps must be taken?

options.A: Add a local storage key in the browser for the Pages URL mapping to your local CM instance
options.B: Configure the Pages application URL in the allowed cross-origin sources in local environment
options.C: Install the Pages application locally on the development machine
options.D: Disable SSL certificate validation for local development

questions.explanation: To enable local development with the cloud-based Pages application, you need to: 1) Create a local storage key in your browser mapping the Pages URL to your local CM instance, and 2) Add the Pages application URL to the allowed cross-origin sources in your local environment configuration. Installing Pages locally (C) is not required as you're using the cloud version. SSL certificate validation (D) is not related to this setup.

questions.question: What are the required parameters when creating a new custom device preview in the Pages application?

options.A: Device width, screen resolution, and operating system
options.B: Device width, icon, and visibility settings
options.C: Device name, screen orientation, and breakpoint value
options.D: Device type, screen size, and browser compatibility

questions.explanation: When creating a custom device preview in Pages, you need to specify exactly three things: the device width, an icon, and its visibility settings. The other options include parameters that are either not required or not relevant to creating device previews in Pages.

questions.question: Which types of components can be used in the Pages application?

options.A: Only components created with the Components application
options.B: Only standard SXA components from the content tree
options.C: Both SXA components from the content tree and components built with the Components application
options.D: Only components specifically designed for Pages

questions.explanation: The Pages application supports two types of components: standard SXA components from the content tree and components built using the 'Components' low-code/no-code application. It's not restricted to just one type of component, allowing for flexibility in component usage.

questions.question: A content editor has modified a data source item used by a shared footer component that appears on 500 pages of your website. What is the correct approach to publish this change to Experience Edge?

options.A: Publish only the data source item
options.B: Publish all 500 pages that use the footer component

questions.explanation: With Experience Edge, when modifying a shared component's data source, publishing the entire website is the most efficient approach. While technically you could publish all 500 pages (option B), publishing the entire site will trigger optimizations. Options A and C are incorrect because Experience Edge requires publishing entire pages, not individual components or data sources.

questions.question: Which statements are true about publishing to Experience Edge?

options.A: Experience Edge retrieves the entire page layout and its data in a single, pre-compiled JSON file
options.B: You can publish individual data source items directly
options.C: The publishing process includes calculating dependencies and resolving them
options.D: Publishing to Edge is a lightweight operation because it only transfers changed items

questions.explanation: Options A and C are correct. Experience Edge works with pre-compiled JSON files containing entire page layouts (A) and the publishing process involves calculating and resolving dependencies (C). Option B is incorrect because you cannot publish individual data sources. Option D is incorrect because publishing to Edge is actually an expensive operation, requiring full page publishes.

questions.question: When working with the GraphQL API in Sitecore XM Cloud, which of the following statements are TRUE?

options.A: GraphQL endpoints use different URLs for different operations like REST APIs do
options.B: GraphQL queries can only be sent using GET requests
options.C: All GraphQL requests are sent to a single endpoint URL
options.D: GraphQL mutations are only used for reading data

questions.explanation: GraphQL in Sitecore XM Cloud always uses a single endpoint URL for all operations, unlike REST APIs where different URLs are used for different actions. All requests are sent via POST (not GET), and mutations are used for writing/changing data (not reading).

questions.question: You need to create a new item remotely in Sitecore XM Cloud using GraphQL. Which of the following parameters are required in the mutation request?
questions.explanation: When creating an item remotely using a GraphQL mutation, all these parameters are required: name (to specify the item name), templateId (to determine the item type), parentId (to specify where to create the item), and language (to specify the item's language version).

questions.question: What authorization is required to access the GraphQL endpoint in a localhost environment?

options.A: A GraphQL Edge Token from the cloud portal
options.B: The ID of a specific Sitecore item
options.D: No authorization is required for localhost

questions.explanation: In a localhost environment, the GraphQL endpoint requires an API key which is the ID of a specific item in Sitecore. The GraphQL Edge Token is used for published cloud environments, not localhost. Authentication is always required, and username/password is not the correct method.

questions.question: A developer notices that the 'Updated By' field value from their content item is not appearing on the published site. What is the most likely explanation?

options.A: The field was not included in the template's field list
options.B: Item statistics fields are not published to Experience Edge
options.C: The template is located in the wrong Helix layer
options.D: The field requires special publishing permissions

questions.explanation: Item statistics fields (including 'Created By', 'Created Date', 'Updated By', and 'Updated Date') are explicitly excluded from publishing to Experience Edge. This is by design to keep the published payload efficient and focused on content-related data rather than system metadata.

questions.question: Which of the following item properties will ALWAYS be published to Experience Edge?
questions.explanation: Item Name, Display Name, and Item ID are always published to Experience Edge as they are essential for item identification and display. Created Date, being an item statistic field, is not published to Experience Edge to maintain efficient payload size.

questions.question: A content editor reports that fields from their new template are not being published. The template contains valid fields but is located in a custom folder at /sitecore/templates/Custom/MyTemplate. What is the solution?

options.A: Move the template to the Foundation, Feature, or Project folder
options.B: Add publishing permissions to the template
options.C: Enable the fields for Experience Edge publishing

questions.explanation: For an item to be publishable to Experience Edge, its template must be located under one of three specific Helix folders: Foundation, Feature, or Project (or in a user-defined folder). Templates in custom folders outside these locations will not be published, regardless of their field configuration or permissions.

questions.question: When developing a new component for XM Cloud, which approach ensures maximum compatibility across editing interfaces?

options.A: Design for Experience Editor first, then adapt for Pages
options.B: Design for Pages first, which ensures Experience Editor compatibility
options.C: Design separately for both Pages and Experience Editor
options.D: Design only for Experience Edge deployment

questions.explanation: The key development principle states that making components compatible with Pages first will ensure they are also compatible with the Experience Editor. This approach provides the most efficient development path and ensures compatibility across interfaces. Other approaches either work backwards (A), create unnecessary duplication (C), or ignore important interfaces (D).

questions.question: Which statements are true about the Frontend as a service wrapper component in XM Cloud?

options.A: It enables Experience Editor to display Experience Edge components
options.B: It requires a data source in the content tree
options.C: It references components using Library ID and Component ID
options.D: It is required for Pages to display Experience Edge components

questions.explanation: The Frontend as a service wrapper component allows Experience Editor to display external components (A) and uses Library ID and Component ID for references (C). It specifically does NOT require a data source in the content tree (B is false). Pages can display Experience Edge components directly without the wrapper (D is false).

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Component Builder in XM Cloud?

options.A: To edit existing components in the content tree
options.B: To create custom C# code for components
options.C: To create and map components to data sources without coding

questions.explanation: The Component Builder is specifically described as a no-code/low-code tool for creating components and mapping them to data sources (C). It is not for editing existing content tree components (A), writing custom code (B), or managing Experience Editor settings (D).

questions.question: A content editor wants to be notified when specific marketing content items are modified. Which combination of features would best accomplish this?

options.A: Content Editor notifications and email alerts
options.B: Webhooks combined with Rules Engine conditions

questions.explanation: Webhooks combined with Rules Engine conditions is the correct solution as it allows for external notifications based on specific item events (content modification) with granular control through rules to target only marketing content items. Other options either don't provide external notifications or lack the specific targeting capability.

questions.question: Which statements about Sitecore XM Cloud webhooks are correct?

options.A: Webhooks can trigger notifications for item lifecycle events
options.B: Rules engine can set conditional criteria for webhook execution
options.C: Webhooks support both basic auth and token authentication
options.D: Webhooks must be triggered for every content change

questions.explanation: A and B are correct because webhooks can notify about lifecycle events and the rules engine provides granular control over triggers. C is incorrect as webhooks currently only support open URLs without authentication. D is incorrect because webhooks can be configured with rules to trigger only under specific conditions, not every change.

questions.question: In the sample workflow, which action is triggered when an item reaches the 'Approved' state?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is webhook submit action. This is specifically used in the sample workflow to notify external systems like Experience Edge when an item reaches the Approved state. A direct publish action is incorrect as the workflow uses webhooks instead. A webhook handler action is incorrect as handlers receive events rather than throw them. Edge publish action is not the correct terminology used in the workflow.

questions.question: Which statements are true about actions and handlers in Sitecore XM Cloud workflows?
questions.explanation: Actions throw events while handlers receive events. This is a fundamental distinction in the workflow system. Options B and D are incorrect because they reverse these roles - handlers do not throw events, and actions do not receive them.

questions.question: What are the states in the sample workflow?
questions.explanation: The sample workflow specifically consists of three states: Draft, Evasion, and Approved. The other options contain incorrect state combinations. While 'Published' and 'Review' might seem logical, they are not part of this specific sample workflow structure.

questions.question: A company has three websites that share the same branding templates but display different content for different regions. What is the recommended rendering host architecture?

options.A: Deploy three separate rendering hosts, one for each website
options.B: Use a single shared rendering host with the Multisite add-on
options.C: Create a hybrid setup with two rendering hosts
options.D: Deploy separate rendering hosts without the Multisite add-on

questions.explanation: Since the websites share the same branding templates (implying a shared code base) but only differ in content, a single shared rendering host with the Multisite add-on is the most efficient solution. Separate rendering hosts would be redundant and less maintainable as the code base is identical.

questions.question: Which factors should be considered when deciding between shared and dedicated rendering hosts?
questions.explanation: The two main factors for choosing between shared and dedicated rendering hosts are branding requirements (A) and code base requirements (B). The number of content editors (C) and geographic location of users (D) don't impact the rendering host architecture decision.

questions.question: A client needs to set up two websites with completely different branding, styling, and functionality. What is the most appropriate rendering host configuration?

options.A: Use a single rendering host with the Multisite add-on
options.C: Use a single rendering host without the Multisite add-on
options.D: Share a rendering host and use CSS overrides

questions.explanation: When sites require unique branding and code bases, dedicated rendering hosts should be used. This allows each site to maintain its own distinct code base, styling, and functionality. Using a shared rendering host (A, C, D) would complicate maintenance and potentially create conflicts in the code base.

questions.question: A developer needs to serialize a template and all its standard values, but NOT its child items. Which scope setting should be used in the .module.json include rule?

options.D: No scope setting needed - it will default to the correct behavior

questions.explanation: When you need to serialize just the item itself without any children or descendants, you should use 'scope: item'. The default scope (no setting) would serialize all descendants, 'item and children' would include immediate children, and 'item and descendant' would include all nested items - both of which would serialize more than needed in this case.

questions.question: Which statements are true about content serialization in Sitecore XM Cloud?

options.A: Items as Resources bundles serialized content into a single .dat file for efficient deployment
options.B: The .sitecore.json file contains the include rules for item serialization
options.C: Serialized content is deployed to the App_Data/items/master folder on the CM instance
options.D: If an item is not specified in an include rule, an error will be thrown during serialization

questions.explanation: Options A and C are correct. XM Cloud uses Items as Resources to bundle content into a .dat file, which is deployed to App_Data/items/master on the CM instance. Option B is incorrect because include rules are defined in .module.json files, not .sitecore.json. Option D is incorrect because items not specified in include rules are simply ignored, not treated as errors.

questions.question: A content editor reports they can't access items they previously could edit after being added to a new role. Which tool should you use first to diagnose this issue?
questions.explanation: Access Viewer is the correct tool for auditing and troubleshooting permissions issues. It allows you to see all permissions affecting an account, including explicit denies that might be overriding other permissions. The Security Editor is for setting permissions, not diagnosing issues. Role Manager and User Manager don't provide detailed permission analysis.

questions.question: Which statements are correct about Sitecore XM Cloud security best practices?

options.A: Assign permissions directly to individual users for precise control
options.B: Permissions should be managed at the role level
options.C: An explicit deny permission takes precedence over allow permissions
options.D: Security Editor is only used for viewing permissions, not setting them

questions.explanation: B and C are correct. Managing permissions at the role level is a best practice for scalability and easier management. It's also true that explicit deny permissions override any allow permissions. A is incorrect because assigning permissions to individual users is harder to manage and not recommended. D is incorrect because the Security Editor is specifically used for setting permissions, not just viewing them.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of components created using the Sitecore XM Cloud Component Builder compared to traditional components?

options.A: They are restricted to a single environment for better security
options.B: They have broader project or organizational scope beyond specific environments
options.C: They can only be used by developers with coding experience
options.D: They require manual style implementation for each instance

questions.explanation: Components created with the Component Builder have a wider project or organizational scope, not being limited to specific environments. This is a key advantage as it allows for greater reusability across the organization. Option A is incorrect as they are not restricted. C is wrong as it's a no-code/low-code tool. D is incorrect because the style library feature ensures automatic style consistency.

questions.question: Which features are provided by the Component Builder in Sitecore XM Cloud?

options.A: Automatic style consistency through the style library feature

questions.explanation: The Component Builder provides three key features: a no-code/low-code interface (D), the ability to predict values for component fields (B), and a style library feature for ensuring visual consistency (A). Option C is incorrect because Component Builder components specifically have a broader scope beyond specific environments.

questions.question: What are the correct stages in order for a Sitecore XM Cloud deployment?
questions.explanation: The correct order of deployment stages in Sitecore XM Cloud is: Provision (where CM, Edge, and Edison host are provisioned), followed by Build, then Deploy, and finally Post-action. This sequence is important for proper deployment flow and must be memorized for the exam.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid methods for deploying to Sitecore XM Cloud?
questions.explanation: There are exactly three ways to deploy to Sitecore XM Cloud: using the Deploy App, using the CLI, and using the REST API. FTP Upload is not a valid deployment method for XM Cloud.

questions.question: A developer needs to delete an entire site collection in Sitecore XM Cloud. What is the correct approach?

options.A: Use the Delete button in the Content Editor
options.D: Manually delete all items in the collection

questions.explanation: When specifically deleting a site collection, you must use the 'Remove Site Collection' script. The 'Remove Site' script is only for individual sites, while manual deletion or using the Delete button are not the recommended approaches for removing site collections.
